channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: A Plea from an Advocate in Mongolia
item.description: Mongolia’s experience shows why tobacco harm reduction must include the voices of advocates working closest to smokers. In countries where smoking rates remain high and cessation support is limited, lower-risk nicotine alternatives can offer a practical route away from combustible tobacco. Ignoring local realities risks leaving smokers with cigarettes as the easiest option. Public health policy should listen to people on the ground and support solutions that reduce harm in real-world conditions. (Published on June 25, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Tiny Pacific Nation Applies for Global Nicotine Ban. Who’s Behind It?
item.description: Palau’s proposal for a global nicotine ban raises serious concerns about proportionality, evidence and unintended consequences. Treating smoke-free nicotine products like illicit drugs ignores the major difference between dependence and the harms caused by combustion. Prohibition would not remove demand — it would push consumers towards illegal markets while cigarettes remain the most dangerous option. Global nicotine policy should reduce harm, not repeat the failures of the war on drugs. (Published on June 25, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: The Test Has Been Run. It’s Time to Mark the Results.
item.description: The evidence from real-world nicotine policy is already in: prohibition and excessive restriction do not eliminate demand, they change where demand goes. Countries that preserve access to safer alternatives see more realistic routes away from smoking, while those that ban or overtax them often strengthen illicit markets. The lesson is clear — regulation should be measured by outcomes, not intentions. It is time for nicotine policy to follow the evidence. (Published on June 25, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Dealing with the health, wellbeing and ethical impacts of prohibition #GFN26
item.description: Prohibition carries health, wellbeing and ethical costs that are often overlooked. When safer nicotine products are restricted, people may be pushed towards cigarettes, illicit markets or unsafe supply chains. Harm reduction offers a more humane approach by reducing risk without demanding perfect abstinence. Public health should protect people in the real world, not punish them for using nicotine. (Published on June 25, 2026bygfn.tv)
